summary: Joel Spolsky reports that during the major 2003 Northeast blackout, his website and company Fog Creek remained online thanks to their colocation provider Peer 1 Network. While many hosting competitors went down, Peer 1 maintained 100% uptime on backup generators. Joel notes that emergencies are when people first discover flaws in their backup plans, and praises Peer 1 for their reliability.
key_points: Emergency backup plans often fail when first tested under real conditions, revealing overlooked dependencies
Peer 1 Network maintained 100% uptime during the 2003 Northeast blackout while competitors failed
Fog Creek continued selling software throughout the blackout thanks to reliable hosting infrastructure
Choosing reliable infrastructure partners matters most during unexpected crises
money_quotes: A once-in-a-generation blackout is the first time most people discover the problems with their emergency backup plans.
Oh, look, we're out of diesel fuel. Or: I didn't know our generator needs electricity to turn on!
We even sold software while the electricity was out.
key_insight: Disaster recovery plans are only as good as their real-world testing, and choosing reliable infrastructure partners pays off when crises actually hit.
